testing-inheritance-and-polymorphism.label: Testing Inheritance and Polymorphism
testing-inheritance-and-polymorphism.summary: Test the shared promise across parent and child classes, then prove a mixed list behaves through the same method call.

try_testing_inheritance_and_polymorphism.title: Test a subclass override
try_testing_inheritance_and_polymorphism.prompt: The lesson used notification classes to explain testing an interface. Now practice with media classes. Complete `build_book_label()` so it returns the label for `Book("Dune", "Frank Herbert")` exactly as `Book: Dune by Frank Herbert`.
try_testing_inheritance_and_polymorphism.hint: The test should prove that the Book subclass override returns the expected label.

help.concept: Testing subclass behavior means checking the exact result that the shared method should return for one concrete subclass.
help.hint_1: Create the `Book` inside `build_book_label()`, then return the result of calling `label()` on that object.
help.hint_2: Do not remove the existing assertion or success print line.

try_testing_inheritance_and_polymorphism.starterCode:
from models.book import Book


def build_book_label():
    # Create the Book and return its label.
    return ""


assert build_book_label() == "Book: Dune by Frank Herbert"
print("book test passed")

tests_check_book_py.content:
from models.book import Book


def build_book_label():
    # Create the Book and return its label.
    return ""


assert build_book_label() == "Book: Dune by Frank Herbert"
print("book test passed")

0.message: build_book_label() should return the Book label from the subclass override.

1.message: Keep the success message print after the assertion passes.

try_testing_inheritance_and_polymorphism.title: Test a polymorphic label service
try_testing_inheritance_and_polymorphism.prompt: Practice a mixed polymorphic result. Complete `build_sample_labels()` so it returns labels for a Book and a Game: `Book: Dune by Frank Herbert` and `Game: Portal 2 on PC`.
try_testing_inheritance_and_polymorphism.hint: Loop through the mixed list and call the same `label()` method on each object.

help.concept: A polymorphism test should prove the same method call works across different subclasses.
help.hint_1: Start with an empty list, append `item.label()` for each object, and return the finished list.
help.hint_2: Do not rewrite the models in `main.py`; the missing logic belongs in the service file.

try_testing_inheritance_and_polymorphism.title: Test validation with a fixture edge case
try_testing_inheritance_and_polymorphism.prompt: Practice an edge case. Complete `build_empty_title_label()` so a game with an empty title falls back to `Untitled` and returns `Game: Untitled on Switch`.
try_testing_inheritance_and_polymorphism.hint: Replace an empty incoming title before the label method uses it.

help.concept: A good test includes edge cases. Here, the subclass should still return a useful label when the title is missing.
help.hint_1: In `__init__`, use a fallback like `"Untitled"` when the incoming title is empty.
help.hint_2: Leave `label()` using the stored `self.title` so the validation change shows up automatically.

policy_single_choice_1.title: Choose what the test should prove
policy_single_choice_1.prompt: When you test `Book.label()`, what is the most useful thing to check?
policy_single_choice_1.hint: Focus on the promised result of the shared method.

help.concept: A good inheritance test checks the behavior callers depend on, not random details around the class.
help.hint_1: Look for the choice about the exact returned label.
help.hint_2: The best answer checks subclass behavior through the shared method.

options.a: That `Book.label()` returns the exact expected string
options.c: That the class uses two blank lines before each method

policy_multi_choice_1.title: Choose good polymorphism test habits
policy_multi_choice_1.prompt: Which habits help when you test a mixed list of `Book` and `Game` objects?
policy_multi_choice_1.hint: Pick the choices that keep the test focused on the shared `label()` contract.

help.concept: A strong polymorphism test checks that one loop can call the same method on different object types and get the right results back.
help.hint_1: Helpful habits keep the list mixed and check the returned labels in order.
help.hint_2: Avoid choices that add type-checking or ignore the test output.

options.a: Build one mixed list and call `label()` on each item
options.b: Check that the returned labels stay in the original order
options.c: Add `if type(...)` checks before every call to `label()`
options.d: Compare the test result with the exact expected labels

policy_drag_reorder_1.title: Order the inheritance test steps
policy_drag_reorder_1.prompt: Put these steps in a sensible order for testing subclass behavior.
policy_drag_reorder_1.hint: Start by creating the object, then call the method, then compare the result.

help.concept: A clean inheritance test creates a concrete subclass object, calls the shared method, and then checks the returned value.
help.hint_1: The object must exist before you can call the method.
help.hint_2: You compare the returned label after the method call.

tokens.t3: Compare the returned string with the expected label

policy_fill_blank_choice_1.title: Choose the parent class name
policy_fill_blank_choice_1.prompt: Fill in the parent class name so `Book` inherits from the shared catalog base class.
policy_fill_blank_choice_1.hint: Use the class that defines the shared `label()` contract.

help.concept: A subclass names its parent class inside the parentheses of the class definition.
help.hint_1: Use the shared item class from this topic.
help.hint_2: Do not pick a method name or a Python keyword.

testing-inheritance-and-polymorphism-ske.title: Test the contract, not every implementation detail
testing-inheritance-and-polymorphism-ske.bodyMarkdown:
When classes share a method, tests should focus on the behavior promised by that method.

Imagine notification classes:

```python
email = EmailNotification("Welcome")
assert email.message() == "Email: Welcome"
```

The test does not need to inspect every line inside the class. It checks the result that callers depend on.

That makes the test useful even if the internal code changes later.

testing-inheritance-and-polymorphism-ske.title: Test a mixed list through the shared method
testing-inheritance-and-polymorphism-ske.bodyMarkdown:
Polymorphism matters most when a helper works with mixed objects.

```python
notifications = [
    EmailNotification("Welcome"),
    SmsNotification("Code sent"),
]

messages = [note.message() for note in notifications]
assert messages == ["Email: Welcome", "SMS: Code sent"]
```

The test proves one loop can trust the shared method name.

testing-inheritance-and-polymorphism-ske.title: Test edge cases near the shared behavior
testing-inheritance-and-polymorphism-ske.bodyMarkdown:
A subclass should still behave clearly when the input is awkward.

```python
message = SmsNotification("")
assert message.message() == "SMS: Untitled"
```

This kind of test protects the interface. It says, "even when data is missing, this object still returns a predictable label."
